title: Fine-Tuning
description: Customize model behavior with data and training strategies before deploying at scale.
outcomes: Decide when fine-tuning is warranted over prompt engineering.
Prepare and clean domain data for instruction tuning.
Understand PEFT concepts and memory-aware methods.
Configure evaluation loops for targeted behavior changes.
Know when to avoid fine-tuning due to risk or cost.

lessons.title: What is Fine-Tuning?
lessons.description: Understand how model behavior can be adapted with examples.
checklist: I can explain fine-tuning vs prompt engineering.
I can identify suitable use cases.
I can name one data quality risk.
questions: When does fine-tuning become the least costly option?
What is the wrong use case for fine-tuning?

lessons.title: Datasets
lessons.description: Build and validate training data for adaptation tasks.
checklist: I can define a schema for training samples.
I can detect low-quality or noisy examples.
I can split train/validation data correctly.
questions: How can bad labels affect behavior?
How much diversity is enough in one domain dataset?

lessons.title: Instruction Data
lessons.description: Use instruction-style examples for controllable output styles.
checklist: I can craft instruction pairs with consistent style.
I can include edge cases and rejection examples.
I can document expected output format.
questions: What makes one instruction sample stronger than another?
Why do failure examples matter?

lessons.title: LoRA
lessons.description: Apply low-rank adaptation for efficient parameter updates.
checklist: I can explain LoRA’s efficiency advantage.
I can identify common rank tradeoffs.
I can describe where adapters are inserted.
questions: Why does LoRA reduce compute in many adaptation cases?
Where can parameter bottlenecks appear?

lessons.title: QLoRA
lessons.description: Use quantized low-rank adapters for lower memory adaptation.
checklist: I can compare LoRA and QLoRA tradeoffs.
I can evaluate memory/quality balance.
I can set practical fallback criteria.
questions: When should you prefer QLoRA over LoRA?
How do you test quality drift after quantization?

lessons.title: Evaluation
lessons.description: Measure adaptation quality using paired prompts and tasks.
checklist: I can define behavioral and quality benchmarks.
I can compare pre- and post-fine-tuning outputs.
I can monitor regressions on core tasks.
questions: How do you avoid overfitting evaluation loops?
What baseline must always be preserved?

lessons.title: When Not to Fine-Tune
lessons.description: Use alternatives when fine-tuning adds cost and risk.
checklist: I can list viable alternatives.
I can assess risk in behavior drift.
I can choose conservative rollout strategies.
questions: When do data changes break a fine-tuned model quickly?
Which tasks should stay prompt-based?

lessons.title: Mini Project: Small Assistant Tuning
lessons.description: Prepare a compact instruction dataset and tuning strategy.
checklist: I can define training and validation splits.
I can document target style and tone constraints.
I can build an evaluation checklist.
questions: How do you reduce overfitting in tiny datasets?
What is your rollback policy?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: Fine-Tune a Small Assistant
lessons.description: Ship a complete adaptation plan for a small assistant.
checklist: I can build a reproducible training and eval pipeline.
I can evaluate before-and-after quality.
I can propose monitoring and rollback.
questions: How will you measure customer value after tuning?
Which safeguards prevent misuse after deployment?

lessons.title: Fine-Tuning Quiz
lessons.description: Confirm readiness to decide on adaptation and deployment tradeoffs.
checklist: I can justify when to fine-tune.
I can design instruction quality checks.
I can balance cost, quality, and risk.
questions: Which tradeoff is strongest in choosing LoRA vs no tuning?
What metrics reveal adaptation failure?

quizQuestions.prompt: When is fine-tuning better than prompt engineering?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Fine-tuning is better when repeated style, policy, or domain behavior must be baked in consistently and prompt methods are too brittle or costly at scale.

options.label: Fine-tune first
options.explanation: Fine-tuning is best when behavior or style must change repeatedly, not as the first fix for missing context.

options.label: Repeated behavior adaptation
options.explanation: Fine-tuning is better when repeated style, policy, or domain behavior must be baked in consistently and prompt methods are too brittle or costly at scale.

options.label: Unreviewed log data
options.explanation: Training data needs consent, cleaning, quality review, and validation splits.

options.label: Same LoRA tradeoffs
options.explanation: They differ in memory, quantization, training cost, and deployment constraints.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are instruction pairs and validation splits central to fine-tuning quality?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: High-quality instruction pairs define exact behaviors, while proper validation catches overfitting and drift before production.

options.label: Quality pairs and validation splits
options.explanation: High-quality instruction pairs define exact behaviors, while proper validation catches overfitting and drift before production.

options.label: Unreviewed log data
options.explanation: Training data needs consent, cleaning, quality review, and validation splits.

options.label: Same LoRA tradeoffs
options.explanation: They differ in memory, quantization, training cost, and deployment constraints.

options.label: No rollback path
options.explanation: Fine-tuned models need rollback, comparison, monitoring, and recovery plans.

quizQuestions.prompt: Compare LoRA and QLoRA from a practical deployment perspective.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: LoRA is simpler and often more stable, while QLoRA reduces memory use through quantization but can introduce quality tradeoffs to watch carefully.

options.label: Stability versus memory savings
options.explanation: LoRA is simpler and often more stable, while QLoRA reduces memory use through quantization but can introduce quality tradeoffs to watch carefully.

options.label: Same LoRA tradeoffs
options.explanation: They differ in memory, quantization, training cost, and deployment constraints.

options.label: No rollback path
options.explanation: Fine-tuned models need rollback, comparison, monitoring, and recovery plans.

options.label: Training-set evaluation
options.explanation: Validation data must be separate to detect memorization and weak generalization.

quizQuestions.prompt: What should a rollback plan include after model adaptation?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A rollback plan needs a baseline version, clear trigger thresholds, automatic fallback path, and a validated re-deployment checklist.

options.label: No rollback path
options.explanation: Fine-tuned models need rollback, comparison, monitoring, and recovery plans.

options.label: Training-set evaluation
options.explanation: Validation data must be separate to detect memorization and weak generalization.

options.label: Baseline, thresholds, rollback
options.explanation: A rollback plan needs a baseline version, clear trigger thresholds, automatic fallback path, and a validated re-deployment checklist.

options.label: Fine-tune changing facts
options.explanation: Changing facts are usually better handled with retrieval or tools.
